docs.title: Turning Requirements Into Results
docs.text:
Business problems are often unclear, messy, and full of assumptions.

And organizations need clear requirements, structured processes, and data they can act on. As a Business Analyst I work to translate business needs into clear documentation, processes, and decisions that move projects forward.

My experience spans healthcare, technology, and enterprise software environments, where I've served as the connective tissue between business stakeholders and technical teams — translating needs into requirements, processes into clear models, and data into decisions that leadership can act on.

Industries: Healthcare · Technology · Finance · Consulting · Insurance

Core Strengths: Requirements Elicitation · Process Modeling · Data Analysis & Visualization · Agile Facilitation · Technical Documentation · Stakeholder Management

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Before moving into business analysis, I was a software test automation engineer scripting automated test cases for SaaS web applications and APIs. That strong background in software development and programming equips me with the ability to understand complex technical concepts and communicate them effectively.

docs.title: Business Analysis Samples
docs.text:
The samples below represent the range of business analysis documentation I produce — from API references and developer guides to SaaS user guides, release notes, and troubleshooting content.

📋 Confidentiality Notice: All projects in this portfolio were completed under non-disclosure agreements. Hence client names, proprietary data, and identifying details have been anonymized. All sample documents, diagrams, and visualizations closely represent the original deliverables but have been recreated for portfolio purposes in compliance with confidentiality obligations.

  • Documentation — BRDs, FRDs, User Stories, Wireframes

    Led requirements documentation for a clinical data system migration — delivering zero compliance findings at launch and 85% staff adoption in month one.

    Read sample →

  • Data Analysis & Dashboards

    Transformed a 5-day manual reporting process into a real-time executive dashboard, giving leadership instant visibility into a $2.4B loan portfolio.

    Read sample →

  • Problem-to-Solution Narratives — Agile, SWOT & GAP Analysis

    Recovered a stalled digital transformation using SWOT and GAP analysis, delivering Phase 1 in 11 weeks and achieving 91% voluntary tool adoption.

    Read sample →

  • Business Process Modeling — BPMN & UML

    Mapped an undocumented claims process for the first time, cutting average cycle time from 42 to 23 days — a 45% reduction.

    Read sample →

  • End-to-End Case Study

    Rebuilt a SaaS company's fragmented knowledge infrastructure, reducing support tickets by 30% and cutting employee onboarding time nearly in half.

    Read sample →

docs.title: From Scattered Data to a Single Source of Truth
docs.text: Rebuilding a SaaS Company's Knowledge Infrastructure Before It Became a Growth Liability

docs.title: The Challenge
docs.text:
A fast-growing SaaS company had a knowledge problem hiding in plain sight. Product documentation lived in email threads, shared drives, outdated wikis, and the institutional memory of engineers who had been with the company since its earliest days. As the team scaled past 150 employees and the customer base expanded, support ticket volume climbed — not because the product was failing, but because nobody could find reliable answers fast enough.

The company was spending money it didn't need to spend. Customers were frustrated in entirely preventable ways. Leadership knew something had to change, but didn't know where to start.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text: I was engaged as a Business Analyst embedded with the Product and Customer Success teams. My mandate was to assess the current state of the knowledge infrastructure, identify critical gaps, and design a scalable solution with requirements the internal team could build and maintain long after the engagement ended.

docs.title: What I Did
docs.text:
The work unfolded in three phases.

Phase 1 — Discover - Conducted 14 stakeholder interviews across Support, Engineering, Product, and Customer Success - Audited 300+ existing documents to assess accuracy, findability, and coverage - Mapped current documentation workflows to surface how content was actually being created, stored, and accessed

Phase 2 — Diagnose - Performed a GAP analysis comparing current documentation capabilities against the needs of a scaling SaaS organization - Identified five critical gaps: no taxonomy, no content ownership model, no review cadence, no single access point, and no format or quality standards - Prioritized gaps using a MoSCoW framework with cross-functional input

Phase 3 — Design - Authored a Business Requirements Document (BRD) covering the new knowledge base architecture and governance model - Designed a content taxonomy and metadata schema for searchability and discoverability - Collaborated with IT to configure a SharePoint-based internal knowledge hub - Developed a Documentation Governance Model defining ownership, review schedules, and quality standards

Current-State Process Map

Customer Issue Reported
        |
        v
Support Agent Searches for Answer
        |
   +----|----+
   |         |
Found    Not Found
   |         |
Resolve   Escalate to Engineering
              |
         Answer buried in email or tribal knowledge
              |
         Manual one-time response (no doc created)
              |
         Same issue recurs next time

docs.title: The Outcome
docs.text:
• Support ticket volume dropped approximately 30% within 90 days of the new knowledge hub going live
  • New employee onboarding was reduced from 3 weeks to under 10 days
  • The Documentation Governance Model was adopted company-wide as standard operating procedure
  • The support team's NPS contribution improved measurably in the quarter following launch

docs.title: Learning Reflection
docs.text:
The governance model was sound — but it was designed after content migration had already started. In future projects, I'd establish ownership assignments and review cadences before any content moves. Documentation without assigned stewardship drifts toward obsolescence almost immediately, and a lifecycle plan is as important as the content itself.

Tools Used: SharePoint · Lucidchart · Confluence · Microsoft Excel · Miro · Word

docs.title: Turning Loan Portfolio Data into Executive Clarity
docs.text: From a 5-Day Manual Process to a 4-Hour Dashboard-Driven Quarterly Review

docs.title: The Challenge
docs.text:
A regional bank's Risk Management team was preparing its quarterly portfolio reviews the hard way — two analysts spending nearly a full week pulling data from multiple systems, consolidating it across spreadsheets, and assembling static slides for executive review. The process was exhausting, error-prone, and always running against the clock. And by the time the report was ready, some of the data was already stale.

Senior leadership wanted real-time visibility into loan performance, delinquency trends, and sector concentration risk. The current process couldn't deliver it.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text: I served as the Business Analyst and data liaison between the Risk Management team and the IT/Business Intelligence team. My role was to translate the analytical and strategic information needs of multiple stakeholders into a functional, intuitive Power BI solution.

docs.title: What I Did
docs.text:
Discovery & Requirements - Interviewed risk analysts and C-suite executives separately to understand both operational and strategic information needs - Documented requirements in a Dashboard Requirements Specification covering KPIs, data sources, refresh cadence, user roles, and design preferences - Discovered significant data quality issues during exploratory analysis: duplicate records, inconsistent category labels, and missing date fields in the source system

Analysis & Design - Used Python (pandas) and Excel to profile, clean, and document the upstream data issues before dashboard development began - Worked with IT to resolve root-cause data quality problems so the fix would benefit all downstream reports, not just this dashboard - Designed the dashboard architecture with the BI team around four core views

Dashboard Mockup

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  LOAN PORTFOLIO RISK DASHBOARD       Q1 2026 | As of 3/25   ║
╠═══════════════╦══════════════╦═══════════════╦══════════════╣
║ Total         ║ 30-Day       ║ Net Charge-   ║ Avg. LTV     ║
║ Portfolio     ║ Delinquency  ║ Off Rate      ║ Ratio        ║
║ $2.4B         ║ 3.2% (▼0.4%)║ 0.8% (▲0.1%) ║ 71% stable   ║
╠═══════════════╩══════════════╩═══════════════╩══════════════╣
║  DELINQUENCY BY REGION         ║  SECTOR EXPOSURE            ║
║  [Heat map: TX, FL, OH         ║  Real Estate:   38%         ║
║   highlighted amber/red]       ║  Commercial:    27%         ║
║                                ║  Consumer:      22%         ║
║                                ║  Agriculture:   13%         ║
╠════════════════════════════════╩═════════════════════════════╣
║  12-MONTH DELINQUENCY TREND                                  ║
║  Apr─May─Jun─Jul─Aug─Sep─Oct─Nov─Dec─Jan─Feb─Mar            ║
║  3.8  3.6  3.5  3.4  3.6  3.5  3.4  3.3  3.4  3.3  3.2  →  ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

UAT & Iteration - Ran three rounds of user acceptance testing with the risk team - Simplified the design significantly after feedback — the first version was data-dense; the final version surfaced only what drove decisions - Delivered a Data Dictionary and a User Guide to support ongoing self-service use

docs.title: The Outcome
docs.text:
• Quarterly prep time dropped from approximately 5 days to under 4 hours
  • The dashboard was adopted as a standing agenda item in monthly board reviews
  • An early delinquency signal in the trend view triggered proactive borrower outreach in one portfolio segment, reducing projected write-offs
  • Data quality improvements upstream improved the accuracy of three other risk reports that depended on the same source

docs.title: Learning Reflection
docs.text:
Stakeholders often ask for what they think they need rather than what they actually use to make decisions. My first dashboard mockup was overloaded because I took early requirements too literally. I'd introduce a prioritization question earlier in every future engagement: "If you could only look at three numbers before walking into a board meeting, what would they be?" That question alone reshapes the entire design conversation and saves at least one full round of revision.

Tools Used: Power BI · Python (pandas) · Excel · Confluence · Jira · Lucidchart

docs.title: Documentation Samples — BRDs, FRDs, User Stories, Wireframes
Writing the Blueprint for a Clinical Data System Migration
docs.text: When Patient Safety Depends on Getting the Requirements Right the First Time

docs.title: The Challenge
docs.text:
A regional healthcare network was replacing its legacy clinical data management system — a platform more than a decade old, built up with layers of workarounds, custom fields, and undocumented processes nobody fully understood anymore. The replacement needed to preserve data integrity, satisfy HIPAA compliance requirements, and support the day-to-day workflows of nurses, lab technicians, physicians, and administrators — a stakeholder group with competing priorities and almost no time to spare.

The risk of getting requirements wrong wasn't a business inconvenience. It was a patient safety concern.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text: I served as Lead Business Analyst for the requirements phase of the project. My responsibilities spanned stakeholder elicitation, authoring all requirements artifacts, and bridging the clinical teams' needs with the development team's sprint backlog.

docs.title: What I Did
docs.text:
Elicitation - Conducted structured interviews with clinicians, lab technicians, compliance officers, and IT administrators - Ran two facilitated workshops to surface and align requirements across departments with competing priorities - Shifted from open-ended sessions to validation-based sessions — pre-drafting requirements for stakeholders to confirm, challenge, or correct — which dramatically improved both speed and quality

Documentation - Authored the Business Requirements Document (BRD): covering data migration scope, user roles, access controls, reporting requirements, and EHR integration - Produced the Functional Requirements Document (FRD): translating each BRD item into specific, testable system behaviors - Wrote Agile User Stories for development sprint planning - Built wireframes for key UI screens in Miro to validate workflow assumptions before development began - Maintained a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) to ensure every requirement was designed, built, and tested

Sample User Stories

Story ID User Story Acceptance Criteria US-014 As a lab technician, I want to enter specimen results directly into the patient record so that clinicians see real-time data without manual transcription. Results appear in patient record within 60 seconds; no duplicate entry required US-031 As a compliance officer, I want an automated audit log of all record access events so that I can respond to regulatory inquiries within required timeframes. Log captures user ID, timestamp, record accessed, and action taken; exportable to PDF and CSV US-047 As a physician, I want to view a patient's full lab history in a single timeline view so that I can identify trends without navigating multiple screens. Timeline displays all results chronologically; filterable by test type and date range

Wireframe Sample: Lab Results View

+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  PATIENT RECORD — LAB RESULTS                   [Export ▼]  |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Patient: [REDACTED]  |  DOB: --/--/----  |  MRN: ########  |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Filter: [Test Type ▼]      Date Range: [MM/DD] to [MM/DD]  |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Date      |  Test Name       |  Result  |  Normal Range     |
|------------|------------------|----------|-------------------|
|  03/10/26  |  CBC – WBC       |  6.2     |  4.5 – 11.0       |
|  03/10/26  |  Glucose         |  98      |  70 – 100         |
|  02/22/26  |  HbA1c           |  5.7%    |  < 5.7%           |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  [View Full History]                 [Flag for Review]       |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+

docs.title: The Outcome
docs.text:
• System launched on schedule with zero major compliance findings at the post-go-live audit
  • Clinical staff adoption exceeded 85% in the first month — a result the project team attributed to user-centric design driven by thorough requirements work
  • Data entry error rate declined an estimated 22% compared to the legacy system
  • The RTM was cited by the compliance team as one of the most complete they had reviewed on a migration project of this scope

docs.title: Learning Reflection
docs.text:
Clinicians are time-constrained in ways most stakeholders simply are not. My early elicitation sessions were too open-ended, which led to long, unfocused meetings and requirements that needed extensive rework. Switching to validation-based sessions was the turning point. I'd build that approach into my methodology from day one on any healthcare engagement going forward — it respects the stakeholder's time and produces better requirements.

Tools Used: Word (BRD/FRD) · Miro (wireframes) · Jira (user stories and backlog) · Excel (RTM) · Confluence

docs.title: Problem-to-Solution Narratives — Agile, SWOT & GAP Analysis
Rescuing a Stalled Digital Transformation Before It Became a Case Study in What Not to Do
docs.text: When a Strategy Exists on Paper But Not in Practice, the Gap Between Them Has a Cost

docs.title: The Challenge
docs.text:
A mid-sized management consulting firm had made a significant commitment to modernizing its internal delivery model — replacing fragmented project tracking, siloed client reporting, and manual billing reconciliation with an integrated platform. The initiative had executive sponsorship, a vendor selected, and a budget approved. Twelve months in, almost nothing had been delivered.

Teams were still working in the old tools. The vendor relationship was strained. Morale on the internal transformation team was low. Leadership was starting to ask whether the entire initiative should be abandoned — or whether the problem was fixable.

The answer was that the problem was fixable, but only if someone was willing to honestly diagnose what had actually gone wrong.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text: I was brought in as a Business Analyst and facilitation lead during a project recovery engagement. My remit was to assess the current state, identify root causes of the stall, and co-design a revised delivery roadmap that the organization could realistically execute.

docs.title: What I Did
docs.text:
Phase 1 — Honest Assessment

Before any solution work began, the team needed an unvarnished picture of where things stood. I facilitated a structured SWOT Analysis with the transformation steering committee and core delivery team — separately first, then together. Running them separately was deliberate: it gave each group room to be candid before bringing perspectives into the same room.

SWOT Summary

┌─────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┐
│         STRENGTHS           │           WEAKNESSES             │
│ • Executive sponsorship     │ • No dedicated BA or PM resource │
│   still intact              │   assigned to the project        │
│ • Vendor platform is        │ • Requirements were never        │
│   technically sound         │   formally documented            │
│ • Internal champions exist  │ • Change management absent       │
│   across 4 of 6 departments │ • Scope expanded 3x without      │
│                             │   corresponding timeline shift   │
├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│        OPPORTUNITIES        │             THREATS              │
│ • Quick wins available in   │ • Key internal champion leaving  │
│   billing workflow module   │   the organization in 60 days   │
│ • Staff frustration with    │ • Vendor contract renewal        │
│   old tools = change        │   decision point in 90 days      │
│   readiness                 │ • Budget reallocation risk if    │
│ • Phased delivery could     │   another quarter passes with    │
│   restore confidence        │   no visible progress            │
└─────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘

Phase 2 — GAP Analysis

With the SWOT grounding the conversation, I led a structured GAP Analysis across five delivery dimensions: requirements clarity, stakeholder alignment, team structure, scope control, and change management. For each dimension, the team assessed current state, defined a realistic target state, and identified the actions needed to close the gap.

DIMENSION            CURRENT STATE         TARGET STATE          GAP ACTION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Requirements         Verbal, undocumented  Documented BRD/FRD    Author baseline docs
                                           signed off by sponsor  in sprint 1

Stakeholder          Siloed; steering      Unified backlog and    Monthly cross-dept
Alignment            committee not         shared visibility      review cadence
                     reviewing backlog

Team Structure       No BA; dev team       BA embedded with       Assign dedicated
                     fielding biz          delivery team          BA immediately
                     questions directly

Scope Control        No formal change      Change request         Implement change
                     process              process with impact     log and approval
                                          analysis required       workflow

Change Management    Not started           Training and comms     Build into sprint
                                          integrated into         planning from
                                          delivery sprints        sprint 2 forward

Phase 3 — Agile Recovery Roadmap

With root causes identified, I facilitated the design of a Kanban-based recovery roadmap that gave the organization visible forward momentum within the first 30 days — critical for restoring confidence — while protecting space for the foundational requirements work that had been skipped the first time around.

  • Established a Kanban board in Jira with four lanes: Backlog, In Requirements, In Development, and Done
  • Defined WIP limits for each lane to prevent the scope creep that had derailed the original effort
  • Worked with the vendor to re-baseline the delivery schedule around three focused phases rather than one large launch
  • Helped stakeholders celebrate early wins publicly — the billing module went live on time in Phase 1, which changed the narrative around the entire initiative

docs.title: The Outcome
docs.text:
• The initiative moved from effectively stalled to Phase 1 delivered within 11 weeks of the recovery engagement beginning
  • Vendor relationship was preserved; the contract was renewed with a renegotiated scope and timeline
  • The Kanban framework was adopted as the firm's standard delivery tracking approach for all major internal initiatives
  • Internal adoption rate for the billing module hit 91% in the first quarter — the highest voluntary adoption rate of any internal tool rollout in the firm's recent history

docs.title: Learning Reflection
docs.text:
The most important lesson here had nothing to do with a framework or a tool. The project stalled primarily because nobody had been assigned clear accountability for requirements work at the start. Frameworks don't fail — governance vacuums do. In any future engagement involving organizational change of this scale, I would make the assignment of a dedicated BA a formal precondition before any delivery work begins. That single structural decision would have prevented most of what went wrong.

Tools Used: Jira (Kanban) · Miro (SWOT/GAP facilitation) · Confluence · PowerPoint · Excel · Lucidchart

docs.title: Mapping the Invisible: Redesigning a Claims Process Nobody Had Ever Drawn on Paper
docs.text: You Can't Fix What You Can't See — And Nobody Had Ever Drawn This Process on Paper

docs.title: The Challenge
docs.text:
A property and casualty insurance carrier was experiencing a troubling pattern: claims that should have resolved in under two weeks were routinely stretching to six, eight, or even ten weeks. Customers were frustrated. Adjusters were buried. Compliance was flagging audit findings related to communication delays. And the operations team kept explaining the problem the same way — "it's just how the process works" — which is often the clearest possible signal that a process has never been formally examined.

Leadership commissioned a process improvement initiative with one central goal: find out where claims were getting stuck and design a better path forward.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text: I served as the Business Analyst and process modeling lead. My responsibilities included leading the discovery effort, building the current-state and future-state process models, and working with the operations and IT teams to validate that the proposed redesign was both operationally realistic and technically feasible to support.

docs.title: What I Did
docs.text:
Phase 1 — Process Discovery

No formal process documentation existed. The process lived in the habits, workarounds, and institutional knowledge of the adjusters and operations staff who worked it every day. My first task was to make it visible.

  • Conducted ride-along observation sessions with claims adjusters, reviewing 15 live claims across three adjusters
  • Facilitated three process walkthroughs with cross-functional groups: adjusters, supervisors, legal, and IT
  • Used sticky-note mapping in Miro to capture the "as-is" flow before committing anything to a formal model
  • Identified 11 distinct handoff points in the current process — each one a potential delay

Current-State BPMN — Claims Handling Process (Simplified)

[CLAIMANT]          [INTAKE TEAM]        [ADJUSTER]          [SUPERVISOR]        [LEGAL / COMPLIANCE]
     |                    |                   |                    |                       |
 Files Claim ──────> Intake Form         Assigned Claim          |                       |
                    Logged in System ──────> |                    |                       |
                         |             Review & Validate          |                       |
                         |                   |                    |                       |
                         |           [Decision: Complete?]        |                       |
                         |            /              \            |                       |
                         |          Yes               No          |                       |
                         |           |          Request More      |                       |
                         |           |          Info ──────> Back to Claimant             |
                         |           |          (Wait 7–10 days)                          |
                         |      Estimate                          |                       |
                         |      Prepared                          |                       |
                         |           |                            |                       |
                         |    [Decision: >$25K?] ─── Yes ──> Supervisor Review            |
                         |           |                            |                       |
                         |          No                    [Decision: Approve?]            |
                         |           |                     /           \                  |
                         |      Payment                  Yes            No                |
                         |      Authorized          Approved ───────> Dispute ──────> Legal Review
                         |           |                                                    |
                     Claimant     Payment                                           Escalation
                     Notified     Issued                                            or Settlement

Phase 2 — Bottleneck Analysis

With the current state mapped, I used process mining techniques and historical claims data to quantify where time was actually being lost.

HANDOFF POINT                   AVG. DELAY     ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Intake → Adjuster Assignment     2.3 days       Manual queue, no routing rules
Info Request → Claimant          1.1 days       No standardized communication
                                                template; adjusters drafting manually
Adjuster → Supervisor (>$25K)    4.7 days       Supervisor review queue unmanaged;
                                                no SLA defined
Supervisor → Legal               6.2 days       No formal escalation trigger;
                                                adjuster judgment-based
Legal → Resolution               Variable       No tracking; cases fell into
                                                unstructured email workflow
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Total Avg. Unnecessary Delay:   ~14 days of the ~42-day average cycle time

Phase 3 — Future-State Design

With bottlenecks quantified and root causes understood, I facilitated the design of the future-state process. The redesign wasn't about adding technology — it was about adding structure, accountability, and clarity to a process that had grown organically without either.

Key redesign decisions included: - Automated routing rules for adjuster assignment based on claim type and geography, eliminating the manual queue - Standardized communication templates for information requests with automated follow-up triggers - Defined SLAs at every handoff point with visibility in the claims management system - A structured escalation trigger for legal referrals — rule-based rather than adjuster-dependent — reducing subjective variation

Future-State BPMN (Summary)

[CLAIMANT] → [INTAKE: Auto-logged + routed in <4hrs] → [ADJUSTER: Assigned by rule, not manual queue]
    ↓
[Info Request? → Standardized template sent; auto-follow-up at Day 3 and Day 7]
    ↓
[Estimate → Supervisor queue with defined 48hr SLA for >$25K claims]
    ↓
[Approve → Auto-trigger payment | Dispute → Rule-based legal referral with full case file auto-packaged]
    ↓
[Legal → Tracked in system with milestone dates; no unstructured email workflow]

UML Activity Diagram — Escalation Decision Logic

        [Claim Reviewed by Adjuster]
                    |
         [Dispute Identified?]
           /              \
          No              Yes
          |                |
     [Approve]     [Apply Escalation Rules]
          |                |
     [Issue          Claim Value > $50K?
      Payment]        /         \
                    Yes          No
                     |           |
              [Auto-refer    Adj. attempts
               to Legal]    resolution
                                 |
                          [Resolved?]
                           /       \
                          Yes       No
                           |         |
                      [Close]   [Escalate
                                 to Legal]

docs.title: The Outcome
docs.text:
• Average claims cycle time dropped from 42 days to 23 days in the six months following the redesign implementation — a 45% reduction
  • The auto-routing rule eliminated the manual assignment queue, recovering approximately 2.3 days per claim immediately
  • Compliance audit findings related to communication delays were fully resolved in the next audit cycle
  • Adjuster satisfaction improved measurably — structured escalation rules removed a significant source of ambiguity and stress from their daily work
  • The future-state BPMN models were formally adopted as the carrier's documented standard operating procedure for the claims function — the first time the process had ever been officially documented

docs.title: Learning Reflection
docs.text:
The single most impactful part of this engagement wasn't the redesign — it was the act of making the process visible in the first place. Once the current-state map existed, the bottlenecks were undeniable. Teams that had been debating solutions for months aligned quickly once they were all looking at the same picture. In future process improvement engagements, I'd invest even more time in the current-state modeling phase and make sure every key stakeholder validates the map before any solution conversation begins. Shared visibility is itself a form of alignment.

Tools Used: Lucidchart (BPMN/UML) · Miro (process discovery) · Excel (data analysis) · SQL (claims data querying) · Confluence · Jira
